title: Wax-resist dyeing on wool
subtitle: Applying batik wax to merino fabric or feltwork before dyeing to preserve resist areas
excerpt: Wax resist (batik) works on felted or woven wool by blocking the dye where the wax has penetrated the fibres. The technique requires pre-mordanting the wool with alum before any wax is applied, since a hot mordant bath after waxing would melt the resist. Multiple rounds of wax application and dyeing build up layers of colour. Wax is removed with newsprint and a warm iron.
sourceNotes: Batik technique on wool is documented in multiple UK natural dye and textile arts sources. Paraffin-beeswax blends as a resist on wool feltwork is consistent with studio textile practice. Observation-led.

glossaryTerms.definition: A mineral or plant compound used to fix natural dye to fibre. Alum (potassium aluminium sulphate) is the most widely used mordant for wool. For wax-resist dyeing, alum mordanting must be completed before any wax is applied, since a hot mordant bath after waxing would melt the resist.

A technique in which molten wax is applied to fabric before dyeing to block colour from reaching the covered areas. The wax must penetrate the fibre rather than merely coating the surface. After dyeing, the wax is removed by sandwiching the fabric between absorbent paper and pressing with a warm iron.

A small copper-bowled tool with a narrow spout used to apply molten wax in fine lines and controlled dots. The bowl holds the wax at working temperature; the spout controls the flow. A tjanting is used for detailed batik work; a brush is used for larger areas.
The dry weight of the fibre or fabric before any processing, used as the reference unit for calculating mordant quantities. A 15% WOF alum mordant on 100 g of wool fabric means 15 g of alum potash.

content.text: Wax
resist
dyeing (batik) works on felted or woven wool by blocking the dye where molten wax has penetrated the fibre. Applied with a brush for large areas or a
tjanting
for fine lines, the wax must be hot enough to soak into the fibre rather than sitting on the surface as a coating. The critical sequencing rule: the wool must be pre-
mordanted
with alum at 15%
weight of fibre

(WOF) before any wax is applied. A hot mordant bath applied after waxing melts the resist and loses the pattern. Once mordanted and dry, the waxed piece is dyed in a cool or room-temperature natural dye bath; heat is avoided to preserve the resist. Multiple rounds of wax application and dyeing build up overlapping layers of colour.

attrs.title: Mordant safety
attrs.body: Work outdoors or with an extractor fan. Dedicated pots only. Gloves, apron, closed-toe shoes. Keep children and pets out of the dye area.

attrs.title: Wax temperature safety
attrs.body: Melt batik wax in a dedicated pot on low heat. Above 100°C wax smokes and above 150°C it can ignite. Never leave melting wax unattended.

items.name: Wool fabric (woven or felted), dry weight
Natural dye bath, prepared and cooled (e.g. weld or chamomile extract)
Stainless steel dye pot (dedicated, for mordanting)
Newsprint or unprinted newspaper (for wax removal)

content.text: Method
Step 1: Pre-mordant the wool

Soak the dry wool fabric in warm water for 30 minutes until fully saturated. Dissolve alum potash at 15% WOF in a cup of hot water and add to the dye pot with 4 litres of water. Lower the wet fabric into the pot. Heat to 80°C over 30 minutes, stirring gently. Hold at 80°C for 45 minutes. Remove from heat and allow the fabric to cool in the mordant liquor. Lift, squeeze gently, and spread flat to dry completely. Do not apply wax to damp fabric: moisture prevents the wax from penetrating the fibres.
Step 2: Apply the wax resist

Melt the batik wax pellets in a dedicated small pot on a low heat ring. Work in a ventilated space. Hold the temperature at 80 to 90°C using a thermometer: at this temperature the wax is fluid enough to penetrate wool fibres. A loaded brush should flow smoothly without dragging. Apply the molten wax to the dry, mordanted wool fabric in the desired pattern. Work quickly as the wax sets fast.

Check the underside of the fabric: wax that has penetrated the fibre will appear translucent on the reverse. Wax that remains opaque on the reverse has only coated the surface and will not hold a reliable resist. Apply more wax from the underside if necessary.
Step 3: Prepare the dye bath

Prepare a cool or room-temperature natural dye bath. A chamomile extract, weld extract, or dilute onion skin extract works well. Allow the dye bath to cool to below 40°C before adding the waxed fabric. Hot dye baths melt the resist.
Step 4: Dye bath

Submerge the waxed fabric in the cool dye bath. Leave to soak for 1 to 4 hours at room temperature, turning the piece occasionally to ensure even colour coverage of the un-waxed areas. Do not heat the dye bath above 40°C. Lift the fabric and rinse in cool water. The waxed areas will appear lighter or the original mordanted-wool colour where the dye was blocked.
Step 5: Remove the wax and dry

Allow the fabric to dry completely. Place the piece between two sheets of newsprint or unprinted newspaper on an ironing board. Press with a warm iron (wool setting, approximately 150°C): the heat melts the wax and the paper absorbs it. Move to fresh paper and repeat until no more wax transfers. A final rinse in warm water with a small amount of wool wash removes the last traces.
Colour variations

A single wax application followed by one cool dye bath produces a two-tone result. The waxed areas hold the original wool colour, a pale straw or whichever colour was already on the fabric. The open areas take the dye.

Layered colour: after removing the first layer of wax, apply fresh wax to different areas, then dye again in a second, darker dye bath. Each round adds a new colour layer. Dye from pale to dark: once a dark dye covers an area it cannot be lightened.

Crackle effect: allow the wax to cool and set completely, then gently scrunch the fabric before dipping it in the dye bath. The cracked wax allows fine lines of colour to penetrate the formerly blocked areas, producing a characteristic crackle texture.
Troubleshooting

items.problem: The dye bled under the wax and there are no clean resist edges
items.cause: The wax was not hot enough to penetrate the fibres, or the fabric was damp when the wax was applied
items.fix: Ensure the fabric is completely dry before waxing. Heat the wax to 85°C and check that it flows easily without dragging. Confirm the wax appears translucent on the reverse of the fabric, not opaque.

items.problem: The wax did not come out cleanly and the fabric feels stiff with residue
items.cause: The iron was not warm enough, or the newsprint became saturated and could not absorb more wax
items.fix: Use fresh sheets of newsprint frequently throughout the ironing process. If residue persists, a brief soak in warm water with a small amount of wool wash will help. Avoid high iron temperatures that could felt the wool; the wool setting on a domestic iron is sufficient.

items.problem: The un-waxed areas are very pale and the dye colour is barely visible
items.cause: The cool dye bath was too dilute, or the soaking time was too short
items.fix: Prepare a more concentrated dye extract for cool-bath work, since cool baths transfer less colour than heated ones. Extend the soaking time to 4 to 6 hours. Alternatively, use a natural dye with strong colour depth at low temperatures, such as onion skin extract.
